nativeRange.habitat: Found across a range of reef habitats in the western Indo-Pacific, from shallow lagoons to reef slopes to approximately 30 m depth. Derives its specific epithet from Lord Howe Island, the southernmost Pacific coral reef.

decorPreferences: stable base with spacing from neighboring corals

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Has been documented benefiting from target (spot) feeding once or twice per week with meaty frozen foods such as mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, cyclops, and small zooplankton. Feeding tentacles generally extend at night; target feeding via pipette or turkey baster is commonly advised.

avoidWith: corals placed within sweeper tentacle range (~5–7 cm)
aggressive LPS corals without buffer spacing
other Micromussa colonies without 10+ cm buffer

imageCandidates.notes: Susan Prior; ~2048px; in-situ wild colony showing vivid orange corallites with lime-green/cream fleshy ridges — the classic multicolored 'Acan Lord' look, sharp and well-lit.
Susan Prior; ~1500px; sharp in-situ colony macro, deep orange/red corallites with cream tuberculate ridges — different morph coloration for variety.
Filed under accepted synonym Acanthastrea lordhowensis; 500x476 (low-res); crisp single fleshy polyp, orange body with green oral disc and white-tipped tentacles — good polyp-detail close-up.

summary: The Micromussa Coral — also sold as the Aussie Lord or Acan Lord — is a large polyp stony coral (Micromussa lordhowensis) prized for its vivid, multicolored morphs. Intermediate in care, it generally does well under moderate lighting and gentle, indirect flow, and tends to benefit from weekly spot feeding with meaty frozen foods.
careNotes:
The Micromussa Coral was originally described as Acanthastrea lordhowensis (Veron & Pichon, 1982) before being reclassified to Micromussa lordhowensis following morphological and molecular phylogenetic analysis published in 2016, which showed it was more closely related to other Micromussa species than to the genus type Acanthastrea echinata. The genus Micromussa belongs to family Lobophylliidae (Order Scleractinia). The trade names 'Acan Lord' and 'Aussie Lord' persist in the hobby and refer exclusively to this species. For the closely related true Acanthastrea, see [acanthastrea-coral], which covers A. echinata — a distinct species no longer grouped with Micromussa lordhowensis. The genus Acanthastrea was reduced to approximately eight species following the reclassification.

A second species, Micromussa amakusensis (Veron, 1990), is also traded under the 'Micromussa' or 'Acan' umbrella and has a similar distribution and overlapping care requirements. It tends to have smaller corallites than M. lordhowensis and is sometimes sold alongside it without distinction.

Key care notes:
• Lighting: Moderate PAR is generally advised — commonly cited around 75–200 PAR. A primary target of 50–100 PAR is commonly recommended, with tolerance up to 200 PAR for acclimated specimens. Excessive light has been reported to damage tissue; acclimate new specimens by placing lower in the tank and gradually moving to the desired position over 2–4 weeks.
• Flow: Low to moderate, indirect flow is preferred. Direct high flow has been reported to cause polyp retraction or tissue tears. Gentle alternating or random flow that keeps the colony clean without stressing tissue is commonly recommended.
• Placement: Lower to middle areas of the aquarium are generally advised, away from direct current impact and high-intensity light zones.
• Aggression: Sweeper tentacles extend approximately 5–7 cm (2–3 inches) at night and are capable of stinging neighboring corals. A minimum buffer of 10 cm from other specimens is commonly advised, with more space as the colony grows.
• Feeding: Target (spot) feeding once or twice per week is generally considered beneficial, with meaty frozen foods such as mysis shrimp delivered via pipette. The species has been documented as an aggressive feeder with polyps that actively accept presented food.
• Colony size: Wild colonies have been documented forming low clumps up to two metres across. In home aquaria, typical colonies grow to 5–50 cm depending on tank size and care. Designer color morphs command premium prices in the trade.
• Fragging: Colonies can be fragged with bone cutters or a bandsaw. Frags readily encrust and grow on frag plugs or rubble. Fragging is the primary propagation method in captivity; the species is generally considered fast-growing relative to many other LPS corals.
breedingNotes: Fragging (cutting colonies apart) is the primary propagation method in captivity. Colonies can be cut with bone cutters or a bandsaw, and frags readily encrust and grow on frag plugs or rubble. Relative to many other LPS corals, Micromussa lordhowensis is generally considered a fast grower under good conditions. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural reefs; deliberate captive sexual breeding is not commonly practiced in the hobby.

additional.name: ReefChasers – Micromussa lordhowensis Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (77–84°F vs LiveAquaria 72–78°F — averaged to 24–27°C), lighting PAR (50–100 primary, up to 200 acclimated), calcium (400–450 ppm), alkalinity (8–9 dKH), care level (Moderate vs LiveAquaria Easy — averaged to intermediate), and sweeper tentacle reach (2–3 inches / 5–7.5 cm). Temperature ranges disagree significantly; averaged value used, flagged in careNotes.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Micromussa lordhowensis
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Order: Scleractinia, Family: Lobophylliidae), native range (western Indo-Pacific: Red Sea through East China Sea, Japan, Australia), depth range (to ~30 m), colony size (low clumps up to 2 m across), corallite diameter (to 15 mm), and confirmation of 2016 Arrigoni et al. reclassification from Acanthastrea lordhowensis. Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Micromussa (genus)
additional.notes: Used for genus-level taxonomy confirmation (Family Lobophylliidae, Order Scleractinia) and species list. Confirmed M. lordhowensis and M. amakusensis as the two primary traded species. LiveAquaria listed family as 'Mussidae' (outdated pre-2016 classification); Lobophylliidae adopted per Wikipedia and current WoRMS taxonomy.
